title: The Affair

jokes.text: John admits he was the last to use the car but doesn't have the keys — his own sign-out sheet indicts him
jokes.setup: Moira asks if John has the car keys; John says no but admits the sign-out sheet shows he was last to use the car
jokes.punchline: Moira: 'Well, that's not very helpful, John.' — His own bureaucratic system has made him the prime suspect while solving nothing
jokes.explanation: A clean character joke — John's love of systems immediately bites him. The punchline is understated and Moira's dry dismissal lands well. Strong character integration because only John would have a sign-out sheet.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You realize you're the only one actually using this system, don't you?

jokes.text: John notices Stevie's name on the sign-out sheet and questions why she has access when she has her own car
jokes.setup: John is defending the legitimacy of his sign-out sheet
jokes.punchline: He immediately derails into puzzlement over Stevie's name, completely forgetting the original dispute
jokes.explanation: John getting distracted by a tangential bureaucratic mystery is very true to character. Not a huge laugh but adds texture and rings true. A quiet character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Why isn't your name on the sheet?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Because he's not a real employee, he's family.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because I'm not a real employee, I'm family.

jokes.text: David reveals he has the keys — and when asked why his name isn't on the sheet, says 'Because you're the only one using that sign-out sheet'
jokes.setup: John has been defending his sign-out sheet system as the solution to household disputes
jokes.punchline: David's blunt dismissal — the same line Moira used — confirms the sheet is universally mocked and completely useless
jokes.explanation: The joke earns its laugh through structure: the exact same dismissal from a second family member amplifies the joke exponentially. Economy is excellent — David's line is identical to Moira's, which is the whole point. The callback is tight and deliberate.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I can give you a ride if you want.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, that's very kind of you, Roland, but I couldn't impose.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It's no problem at all. I've got some great music we can listen to.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh god, what is that sound?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's Nickelback.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Pull over. I'll walk.

jokes.text: Moira refuses to ride four hours with Roland and Lynyrd Skynyrd
jokes.setup: Moira explains why she doesn't want Roland to drive her to the conference
jokes.punchline: The specific detail of 'Lynyrd Skynyrd' as the unbearable element perfectly characterizes both Roland and Moira's horror at his taste
jokes.explanation: The specific band name does tremendous work — Lynyrd Skynyrd perfectly evokes Roland's character without needing explanation. Moira's aristocratic horror at four hours of Southern rock is completely in-character and efficiently delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know, it's like the safety demonstration on an airplane. They tell you to put on your own oxygen mask before helping others. I need to put on my oxygen mask first.

jokes.text: David argues parents are supposed to put their children before themselves; Moira invokes airplane safety videos — 'a mother puts her own mask on first'
jokes.setup: David appeals to parental duty as justification for taking the car
jokes.punchline: Moira weaponizes the oxygen mask safety instruction as genuine parenting philosophy — a hilariously self-serving misappropriation of safety logic
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The airplane mask logic is a real argument that has been twisted into a perfectly Moira-ish justification for selfishness. It's not random — it's a genuinely coherent (if absurd) application of real safety instructions. Only Moira would cite airline protocol as a parenting framework. High quotability, strong structure, and completely earned by her character.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, my system isn't useless. It's organized. It's methodical. It makes perfect sense.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, your system is a filing cabinet in the garage with a label maker and nothing in it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Exactly. See? It's working.

jokes.text: John reads David's sign-out sheet entry aloud: under 'purpose of trip' David wrote 'driving'
jokes.setup: John has been championing his sign-out sheet as a sophisticated dispute-resolution system
jokes.punchline: David's entry 'driving' as the purpose of the trip is the most useless, tautological answer possible — it shows the sheet is both pedantic and pointless
jokes.explanation: A perfect button on the sign-out sheet running gag. 'Driving' as the purpose of a car trip is beautifully stupid — it's David passive-aggressively complying while also proving the sheet is useless. John's exasperated reading of it is the cherry on top. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I need to file a complaint about this system. It's completely broken.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, the form asks for your name, so I assume you'll be writing 'Johnny Rose' in that field.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, David, that's how forms work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And then it asks for the nature of your complaint, so you'll describe the problem in excruciating detail, I'm sure.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's the entire point of a complaint form.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm, and then you sign it and date it. I mean, I'm just reading what it says.

jokes.text: Roland enthusiastically wants to see Moira's reaction walking into her first RAMC conference — 'And there it is'
jokes.setup: Roland has been building up the RAMC conference as a major event
jokes.punchline: His dramatic staging of Moira's 'reaction' — and the implication that the room is profoundly underwhelming — is the joke; Roland's genuine excitement about something mundane
jokes.explanation: Roland's child-like excitement about a municipal conference is consistent characterization. The joke lands mostly on his sincere enthusiasm for something objectively boring.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: This conference is going to put Schitt's Creek on the map. We're talking about attracting major businesses, investors, dignitaries from across the province.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I've already booked the community center for three days. We'll have keynote speakers, networking events, a gala dinner.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: This is going to be the largest gathering of municipal importance this town has ever seen. People will be talking about this for years.

jokes.text: Moira refuses a name tag because she won't wear anything with an adhesive backing
jokes.setup: Roland tries to get Moira to put on a conference name tag
jokes.punchline: Moira's objection is not to the name tag concept but to the adhesive — a hilariously specific aristocratic refusal
jokes.explanation: Moira's objection is perfectly calibrated — not 'I won't wear a name tag' (too blunt) but 'I won't wear anything with adhesive backing' (specific, absurd, consistent with her character's physical vanity). Economy is tight.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I got some name tags for everyone.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what kind of adhesive are we talking about here?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The regular kind.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I'm afraid regular adhesive is going to compromise the integrity of my outfit. Unless it's medical-grade, hypoallergenic, and cruelty-free, I simply cannot participate.

jokes.text: Roland points out 'the well-dressed one' as Mayor Gavin McCrae — Moira notes he's serving drinks to people; Roland backtracks to 'the other guy over there'
jokes.setup: Roland tries to impress Moira by pointing out the important, well-dressed mayor
jokes.punchline: The 'well-dressed' man is a waiter; Roland's embarrassed pivot to 'also very well dressed' other man is equally doomed
jokes.explanation: Classic two-part embarrassment joke — first wrong identification, then the immediate second attempt implies that one's wrong too. Moira's dry 'Now you've identified the catering staff' is a sharp button. Good structure.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Look at the mayor. Well-dressed fellow.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's not the mayor, Roland. That's catering staff.

jokes.text: Roland lists the terrifying powers of RAMC: road salting, quarry inspection, water tower repair — 'It's really kinda scary that we're all in here under one roof'
jokes.setup: Roland is trying to convey the awesome power and importance of the municipalities conference
jokes.punchline: The list of mundane civic duties is delivered with the gravitas of a NATO summit — the gap between Roland's reverence and the actual stakes is the joke
jokes.explanation: Roland's sincere civic pride is consistently funny. The specific list (road salting, quarry inspection, water tower repair) is well-chosen — each item is more mundane than the last, building to 'frightening.' Solid character work.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's the mayor, right?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, Roland. That's a picture of a leaf.

jokes.text: Moira boasts she once hosted 'the non-televised portion of the People's Choice Awards'
jokes.setup: Moira reassures Roland she knows how to work a room of fragile egos
jokes.punchline: Her credential — the non-televised portion — is a perfect encapsulation of her faded-star status; it's a real credit that is also a humiliation
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. 'The non-televised portion' does enormous work in three words — it simultaneously conveys that she has hosted awards shows (something) AND that she didn't make the cut for the part anyone watched (nothing). The joke requires no explanation, it's instantly and permanently characterizing, and it's infinitely quotable. Only Moira Rose would cite this with genuine pride. Exceptional economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, municipal officials, they're the backbone of this town. Without them, we'd have chaos. Pure chaos.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: But there's one thing that terrifies me more than anything in this world... a gathering of municipal workers. All in one place. At the same time.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The paperwork alone could destroy us all.

jokes.text: David condescendingly dismisses Alexis's business input as things she's 'very excited about' from 'your high school intro to business course'
jokes.setup: Alexis offers what is actually reasonable business advice about brand recognition and synergy
jokes.punchline: David's withering dismissal of correct advice because of its source — and then immediately pivoting to ask for the hand cream she was supposed to get
jokes.explanation: David's reflex to dismiss Alexis even when she's right is solid character comedy. The joke is sharpened by the fact that she's not wrong — which makes his condescension funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh Roland, darling, managing egos is what I do. I've spent years in the entertainment industry dealing with the most volatile, narcissistic personalities imaginable. A little town council presentation? Please. I could do this with my eyes closed and my hands tied behind my back in a Valentino gown.

jokes.text: Alexis insists she got the hand cream — David specifies he wanted THE BOX of hand cream, not to sample it; Alexis: 'That's like 53 pounds'
jokes.setup: David asks Alexis to get the hand cream; she returns having only sampled it
jokes.punchline: Alexis's complaint that the box is 53 pounds reveals she sampled multiple items rather than do any actual physical labor
jokes.explanation: Alexis's physical laziness manifesting as product sampling is well-observed. 'That's like 53 pounds' as an excuse is perfectly in-character — she treats not lifting heavy things as a legitimate reason to disobey instructions.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was once a presenter at the Gemini Awards.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's impressive.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It was the year they ran out of major categories, so they created 'Best Use of Eyeliner in a Television Drama.'

jokes.text: Alexis proposes having her own sub-label under David's brand, selling 'festival wear, hair feathers, and body jewelry'
jokes.setup: Alexis is supposedly helping David at the store
jokes.punchline: She immediately pivots to a self-serving business expansion idea, pitching herself a retail empire within his store
jokes.explanation: Alexis's self-absorption and her specific product ideas (hair feathers, body jewelry) are perfectly calibrated to her character. Functional character beat, not a standout joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, can you get the box of hand cream?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, this box weighs like 50 pounds. Why do you have so much hand cream?

jokes.text: Patrick arrives to drop off the business license; Alexis tells him 'I'm Alexis, and I'm currently studying business and I'm David's sister and life coach'
jokes.setup: A new person (Patrick) has walked in; Alexis introduces herself
jokes.punchline: The title 'life coach' appended to 'sister' — positioning herself as David's professional superior — is a funny overstep given we've just watched her avoid lifting a box
jokes.explanation: Alexis's self-aggrandizing introduction is a clean character joke. The 'life coach' credential is funnier in context — we've just watched her be completely useless. The gap between her self-perception and her behavior is well-established.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Alexis Rose. I'm David's sister, and I'm also his life coach.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Life coach?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, I mean, nobody asked me to do it, but I'm really good at it.

jokes.text: Alexis apologizes to Patrick if her hands are too soft because she's 'been sampling a lot of product' — Patrick confirms they are indeed very soft
jokes.setup: Alexis goes to shake Patrick's hand
jokes.punchline: She preemptively warns him about her hands being too soft, turning her laziness (sampling instead of working) into a flirtatious conversation starter; Patrick's deadpan 'They are' completes it perfectly
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed callback. Alexis's laziness becomes a flirtatious opportunity, which is entirely in character. Patrick's willingness to confirm the hand softness is a deadpan beat that adds a second layer. The rewatch bonus applies because it seeds the Patrick/David dynamic on first introduction.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So, um, have you ever tried the new seasonal latte? It's like, really good. I've been sampling it all morning.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Oh, no, I haven't.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, maybe you should... try it sometime. With me. I mean, not like, together together, but like... we could both be drinking lattes at the same time in the same place?

jokes.text: Reporter calls Moira 'the linchpin'; Roland immediately inserts himself and says he is 'Moira's boss'
jokes.setup: A reporter is interviewing the triumphant Moira about her speech
jokes.punchline: Roland's interjection — 'I'm Roland Schitt, Moira's boss' — is factually true but contextually absurd given she has just outshone him completely
jokes.explanation: Roland's reflexive need to establish authority in the moment Moira has eclipsed him is consistent and funny. The title 'her boss' rings absurd in context.

dialogue.speaker: Reporter
dialogue.line: That was a remarkable speech. How did you prepare for it?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't prepare, per se. I simply allowed my natural eloquence and stage presence to guide me through the moment.

dialogue.speaker: Reporter
dialogue.line: Well, it certainly showed. You're quite the orator.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Thank you. I accept the title of Supreme Orator of our time.

jokes.text: Moira cuts Roland off mid-sentence during his interview attempt: 'Yes, I'll I'm the ingenue here. A total babe in the political woods.'
jokes.setup: Roland is trying to take credit and speak to the reporter
jokes.punchline: Moira steamrolls him by performing false modesty — positioning herself as a naive newcomer while clearly basking in her triumph
jokes.explanation: 'A total babe in the political woods' is a vivid, theatrical phrase that only Moira would use to describe herself. Her fake-humble pivot to redirect the spotlight is spot-on characterization.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I am a political ingénue, a babe in the woods, a fawn-like creature stumbling through the forest of municipal governance on legs that have only just learned to walk.

jokes.text: Roland explains the RAMC cocktail: a sparkling white Russian — 'The trick is to get it down before it curdles'
jokes.setup: Roland invites Moira to the Diamond Club for a special RAMC cocktail
jokes.punchline: The cocktail sounds impressive until the 'curdles' detail — a drink that requires speed to survive suggests it's objectively disgusting; Roland treats this as a helpful tip
jokes.explanation: A tight, economical joke. The sparkling white Russian sounds fancy; 'the trick is to get it down before it curdles' instantly deflates it. Roland's helpful delivery of a deeply unappetizing tip is perfect deadpan. One of the sharper Roland jokes in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: We have a signature cocktail at the diamond club called the Aristocrat's Enema.

jokes.text: The conference crowd chants 'Moira! Moira! Moira!' — Moira shouts over the phone to John that 'the cheering and accolades are drowning out your gentle voice'
jokes.setup: Moira calls John to report on the conference
jokes.punchline: Moira is being literally chanted at by a crowd while on the phone, treating John's desire to speak as an inconvenience competing with her adulation
jokes.explanation: 'The cheering and accolades are drowning out your gentle voice' is a great line — theatrical, affectionate toward John, and self-absorbed all at once. Moira being literally chanted at by a municipal conference crowd while trying to have a phone call is inherently absurd and funny.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not soft, it's cat hair. I'm allergic to cats, so I had to develop a entire product line specifically designed to repel feline fur.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'Well, I don't have my textbook on me.' David: 'I stand corrected.'
jokes.setup: David has just out-jargoned Alexis and Patrick with his retail concept description
jokes.punchline: Patrick's honest admission that he can't counter because he doesn't have the textbook, followed by David's dry 'I stand corrected' — the sarcasm is gentle enough to read as either genuine or cutting
jokes.explanation: The exchange works on two levels — Patrick's literal response and David's ambiguously sarcastic one. It's efficient and establishes their dynamic well. Patrick's earnestness meeting David's snark is the core of their future relationship.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh thank God. I was going to say the frame is absolutely hideous.

jokes.text: Moira on the phone, giddy: 'The cheering and accolades are drowning out your gentle voice.'
jokes.setup: John calls Moira to check in on the conference
jokes.punchline: Moira describes her situation as literally drowning in acclaim — framing crowd noise as 'cheering and accolades' and John's voice as inadequate by comparison
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'drowning out your gentle voice' is particularly good — it's simultaneously affectionate and dismissive of John. Moira's theatrical self-positioning even in a phone call to her husband is perfectly calibrated.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, if you flatten it, it looks like nothing.

jokes.text: John hears the crowd chanting 'Moira!' on the phone and says 'Uh, was that for me? Why am I getting booed?'
jokes.setup: The crowd is chanting Moira's name; John hears it through the phone
jokes.punchline: John interprets the crowd noise as booing directed at him — his mild bewilderment rather than defensiveness is the joke
jokes.explanation: A clean, well-timed joke. John mishearing 'Moira!' as booing and his calm 'Why am I getting booed?' is a lovely deadpan beat. His lack of outrage — just mild confusion — is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, actually, most businesses fail within the first five years.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Yes, which is why I said they need to survive at least a year without profit.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, but they don't. They fail. So... your plan won't work.

jokes.text: Roland grabs the phone and shouts: 'Hey Johnny, it's Roland! Hey, we're all here at RAMC! You should be here, Mary-something is here... Gavin is here... The road salt guy...'
jokes.setup: Roland takes the phone from Moira to shout at John
jokes.punchline: Roland's guest list — 'Mary-something,' 'Gavin,' and 'the road salt guy' — conveys both his drunkenness and his small-town earnestness through the degrading specificity of names
jokes.explanation: 'Mary-something' and 'the road salt guy' are perfectly observed drunk-person details. Roland's excitement about people he can barely name is a funny, efficient character beat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What do the textbooks say about my business model?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Um, I don't know, I didn't go to college.

jokes.text: Moira ends the call with 'Goodbye, phone call!'
jokes.setup: Moira is wrapping up her phone call with John amid chaotic party noise
jokes.punchline: She says goodbye to the phone call itself as an entity, not to John — a magnificently theatrical and slightly drunk non sequitur
jokes.explanation: 'Goodbye, phone call!' is a gem. Only Moira Rose, drunk on success and sparkling white Russians, would say goodbye to the phone call as an abstract object rather than the person on it. It's absurdist but completely character-consistent — Moira treats every moment as theater. Perfectly economical. Highly quotable. A strong contender for best line of the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: There's a man here with a cello, and he's playing it like it owes him money.

jokes.text: Scene cut: Moira is now in bed demanding 'John, shut the blinds' — then 'Johnny, please' — then John is revealed to be Roland
jokes.setup: We see Moira hungover in bed calling out to 'John' — a natural assumption given she's at a hotel with her husband at home
jokes.punchline: The gradual reveal that the person she's been addressing as John is Roland Schitt — naked in his own hotel room — is the episode's biggest structural payoff
jokes.explanation: The scene is structured beautifully across the timestamp gaps — Moira's increasing demands ('shut the blinds,' 'please') build the rhythm before the reveal. The misdirection is earned: we assume she's talking to an unseen hotel employee or even John. Roland's appearance and Moira's 'What the fuck are you doing in my room?' followed by his correction is a textbook reveal structure. High rewatch value.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Hey Johnny, it's Roland. Your wife's a little tied up right now, but I got you. What's going on, buddy? You need anything? I'm here for you.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I... I need to speak with my wife.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, I know, but between you and me, Johnny, I've been meaning to talk to you anyway. How are things at the store? You know, I've been thinking about investing. Maybe we could be partners?

jokes.text: Moira screams 'What the fuck are you doing in my room?!' — Roland: 'What are you talking about? This is MY room.'
jokes.setup: Moira wakes up in what she believes is her room with Roland inexplicably present
jokes.punchline: Roland's correction — it's HIS room — means Moira drunkenly broke into his room, making her the intruder
jokes.explanation: The reversal — Moira is not the wronged party, she's the trespasser — adds a comic layer to the reveal. 'Well, what the fuck are you doing in your room?' (Moira's immediate response) is a perfect escalation: her outrage is so strong she's angry at him for being in his own room.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, this has been lovely, but I must dash. Ciao!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And thank you, Bell telephone apparatus, for your years of service. You've been a true confidant.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Well, what the fuck are you doing in your room?' — her outrage at Roland for being in his own room
jokes.setup: Roland has just told Moira this is his room, not hers
jokes.punchline: Moira's immediate response is still outrage — she's angry at him for being in his own space, which is logically absurd but emotionally Moira
jokes.explanation: This is the sharpest joke in the sequence. The logic is perfect: Roland tells her it's his room; she absorbs this information and her outrage simply pivots to 'what the fuck are you doing in YOUR room?' She cannot conceive of being wrong — her fury redirects immediately. It's absurdist but completely in character. The economy is extraordinary — one line does everything. Strong candidate for the episode's best individual joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Where am I? David? Johnny?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It's me, Roland. You're in my room.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Roland? What are you doing in my bedroom?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: This is my bedroom. You wandered in here last night.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, this is mortifying.

jokes.text: Roland explains Moira pounded on his door asking him to 'whip up a salmon plate,' then flaked out on the bed
jokes.setup: Moira demands to know what Roland is doing there
jokes.punchline: The specific detail of a drunk Moira demanding a 'salmon plate' at whatever hour she knocked on his door is a perfect, absurd character detail
jokes.explanation: Drunk Moira demanding a salmon plate from Roland at midnight is exquisitely specific. It tells you everything about how she conducts herself even when blacked out — still expecting five-star service from whoever is nearby. 'Flaked out on the bed' is a great anticlimactic verb choice.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Wait, this is your room?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yes, it's my room.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, why didn't you say something?

jokes.text: Moira asks if Roland is naked under the sheets; Roland: 'Honey, what I do in the privacy of my sheets is my own business'
jokes.setup: Moira has just realized she's in Roland's bed
jokes.punchline: Roland's dignified defense of his right to be naked in his own bed — while Moira is the trespasser — is an absurd inversion of expected outrage
jokes.explanation: Roland's calm, dignified response about 'the privacy of my sheets' while Moira spirals is a good character beat. His total lack of embarrassment is very Roland — he sleeps naked every night and sees no reason to apologize for it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, no. Please tell me you were wearing something. Anything. A muumuu? A caftan? A tarp?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I sleep in the nude, Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that is the most offensive thing I have ever heard in my entire life.

jokes.text: Moira's repeated 'Oh, God no. No, dear God. Welcome to RAMC. Oh, my God No!' as she processes being in Roland's bed
jokes.setup: The full situation has dawned on Moira
jokes.punchline: 'Welcome to RAMC' inserted mid-spiral is the highlight — a darkly comedic self-commentary as she processes her situation
jokes.explanation: The escalating 'Oh God no' string is well-performed and funny on its own, but 'Welcome to RAMC' mid-spiral is the gem — Moira briefly steps outside her panic to deliver a cutting self-commentary. The callback to Roland's earnest earlier promotion of the conference gives it extra weight.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Are you naked?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, yeah. I sleep naked. It's called freedom, Moira.

jokes.text: Jocelyn tells John 'You're handling this way better than I am' — John has no idea what she's talking about
jokes.setup: Jocelyn approaches John assuming he knows about Roland and Moira sleeping in the same bed
jokes.punchline: John is completely in the dark — he graciously accepts a compliment for handling something he doesn't know about, then gets blindsided
jokes.explanation: Classic dramatic irony setup — we know what Jocelyn means, John doesn't, which makes his gracious acceptance of her compliment funnier. The reveal 'Roland and Moira, sleeping together' landing on John is a clean, well-structured surprise.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Johnny had a really rough morning. He couldn't find his usual coffee mug, so he had to use a different one. And then—get this—it was a mug from the motel gift shop. A motel mug, David. He looked absolutely devastated.

jokes.text: 'Roland and Moira, sleeping together. What?!' — John's delayed processing of the information
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has just told John that Roland and Moira slept together
jokes.punchline: John's 'What?!' followed by 'Let's just go back to that previous sentence' is a perfect slow-burn reaction — measured, confused, not yet panicking
jokes.explanation: 'Let's just go back to that previous sentence' is a great John Rose line — understated, precise, and very him. His measured response to alarming news is consistent with his character as the calm, reasonable one.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, if Moira didn't tell me, she probably had a good reason.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Johnny, she's your wife. She hid something from you. That's suspicious.

jokes.text: John: 'Sleeping with your husband didn't really come up as one of the reasons the night was so successful.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn asks if Moira told John; John says they chatted about what a success the night was
jokes.punchline: John's dry observation that Moira's recap of a successful evening omitted the part where she slept in her husband's boss's bed
jokes.explanation: This is excellent John Rose comedy. The line is perfectly constructed — his understated observation that 'sleeping with your husband' (meaning sleeping with Roland, the grammatically ambiguous husband) wasn't listed as a success metric is layered and precise. The rhythm of the sentence is superb. Strong craft across all dimensions.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, I find it very... stimulating.

jokes.text: John points out Moira 'forgot' to mention sleeping with his husband as one of the reasons 'the night was so successful'
jokes.setup: John explains that he and Moira talked this morning and she described the night as successful
jokes.punchline: He notes with dry precision that 'sleeping with your husband didn't really come up as one of the reasons the night was so successful' — the understatement of the century
jokes.explanation: John's dry observation is very well-written. The construction — 'sleeping with your husband didn't really come up as one of the reasons the night was so successful' — is economical and perfectly captures John's character: he processes upset through wry understatement rather than confrontation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, if I'm going to help you, I think I deserve some sort of compensation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Like, a finder's fee, or... or a kickback, or something.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'We probably shouldn't read anything into the fact that for some reason Moira decided to hide this from you.' John: 'No-no, I wouldn't say 'hide.' No, no.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn notes Moira hid the incident from John
jokes.punchline: John's refusal to use the word 'hide' while clearly knowing that's exactly what happened — his verbal gymnastics around the uncomfortable truth — is a great character beat
jokes.explanation: John's denial and semantic quibbling over 'hide' is consistent with his gracious, glass-half-full character. He can't quite admit the obvious, which is both funny and touching.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I've been putting in sweat equity at the store.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, you arrived at 11:47 this morning and left at 12:03. That's not sweat equity, that's just... sweating while being present.

jokes.text: Jocelyn says she knows what it's like to be in bed with a naked Roland Schitt — 'and I am powerless against that'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is trying to explain why Moira might have been compromised
jokes.punchline: 'I am powerless against that' — Jocelyn's frank admission that she finds her own husband irresistible is both weirdly sweet and deeply funny given how Roland is presented to the audience
jokes.explanation: This is a quietly brilliant joke. Jocelyn, trying to be sympathetic, reveals that she herself is powerless against naked Roland — which the audience finds horrifying but she clearly finds natural. The gap between what the audience sees (Roland as ridiculous) and what Jocelyn experiences (Roland as irresistible) is the comedy. 'I am powerless against that' is perfectly deadpan. Great character specificity — only Jocelyn would say this.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, David, I think what you're experiencing is a classic case of... sweat equity evaporation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's when your sweat stops being equitable. It's actually in the business bylaws.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And honestly, I think you need to file a Form 47-B, which is the official sweat equity correction form.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I learned about that at business camp.

jokes.text: John: 'He was naked?' Jocelyn: 'He sleeps naked every night.'
jokes.setup: John has just learned Roland was naked in bed
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's matter-of-fact confirmation — 'He sleeps naked every night' — delivered as if this is obvious information John should already have, is a perfect deadpan beat
jokes.explanation: Economy is near-perfect here. Jocelyn delivers this as established fact, not revelation — she's slightly confused that John needed to ask. It deepens both characters: Roland's commitment to nudity and Jocelyn's total normalization of it. The short exchange is a great two-hander.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick? Patrick wouldn't be interested in me. Have you seen what he wears?

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'Do you wanna order something to put chocolate sauce on?' — a non-sequitur comfort food offer mid-emotional crisis
jokes.setup: John and Jocelyn are processing the emotionally heavy news about Roland and Moira
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's solution to emotional distress is to order something for chocolate sauce — the weirdly specific comfort is funny, as is the phrasing 'something to put chocolate sauce on' rather than naming a food
jokes.explanation: The phrasing 'something to put chocolate sauce on' rather than 'some chocolate cake' or 'ice cream' is what makes this great. It's open-ended comfort food ordering, suggesting Jocelyn uses this coping mechanism regularly enough that the vehicle is fungible. John's flat 'No' is the perfect beat. Very quotable and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You slept with Roland?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but it was ages ago, before we even knew who he was. He was just some man at a thing.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What? It was hardly memorable. He has a very small—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I don't want to hear this.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, it was a forgettable evening. Besides, look how well things turned out. Now we're neighbors with the man!

jokes.text: Alexis tells David she can 'swing by the store and help out after school' — and mentions she might need more of the lip balm she liked
jokes.setup: Alexis offers to help David at the store
jokes.punchline: The offer of help is immediately undermined by the condition — she needs the lip balm she's been sampling — revealing her 'help' is transactional and self-serving
jokes.explanation: A functional callback to the sampling joke, reinforcing Alexis's character. Not a standout joke on its own but serves the episode's running thread.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We never discussed it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I was embarrassed.

jokes.text: Alexis explains 'sweat equity' — when you help someone and they give you 'lip balm or whatever else you might need'
jokes.setup: Alexis is justifying her demand for the lip balm as payment
jokes.punchline: She has correctly identified the concept of sweat equity but redefined it as 'you help someone and they give you lip balm' — a perfect comic misapplication of a real business term
jokes.explanation: Alexis's business class knowledge applied entirely to her own cosmetic needs is a great character joke. 'Lip balm or whatever else you might need' as the definition of sweat equity is both wrong and internally logical in the Alexis-verse. The 'in exchange for your time and energy' coda adds to the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm pouring this out as a symbolic gesture of my commitment to sobriety.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's my beer.

jokes.text: David: 'I think you might actually need to break a sweat in order to earn sweat equity'
jokes.setup: Alexis has defined sweat equity as receiving lip balm in exchange for helping
jokes.punchline: David's correction is obvious but delivered with perfect timing — 'you might actually need to break a sweat' is a gentle devastating refutation
jokes.explanation: A solid David correction. 'You might actually need to break a sweat' is obvious but beautifully timed. The joke works because David saying the obvious thing is always funnier than it should be.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, you're wasting my beer.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not wasting it, I'm using it as a toner. My aesthetician is on vacation, and my pores have staged a coup.

jokes.text: Alexis proposes outsourcing her sweat equity to Patrick
jokes.setup: David has told Alexis she needs to actually break a sweat herself
jokes.punchline: Alexis proposes having Patrick do the sweating while she takes the credit — correctly identifying the term 'outsourcing' as she applies it to avoid all effort
jokes.explanation: The escalation from sweat equity to outsourcing is wonderful — Alexis keeps correctly using business terms while applying them entirely to avoiding all personal effort. Her confidence in proposing this is peak Alexis. 'That's called outsourcing, outsourcing...' trailing off is a great comedic beat.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I have a confession to make. I chose that frame specifically because you criticized it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I wanted to see your reaction. I think I enjoy annoying you.

jokes.text: David: 'Are you passing this economics course?' Alexis: 'Yes. Yeah. Yes, I'm pretty sure.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has been using business terms to justify avoiding all effort
jokes.punchline: Her three-part confirmation — 'Yes. Yeah. Yes, I'm pretty sure' — undermines itself completely; the qualifier 'I'm pretty sure' renders the preceding yeses meaningless
jokes.explanation: A great three-beat answer. The rhythm of 'Yes. Yeah. Yes, I'm pretty sure' is perfectly calibrated — each successive affirmation is slightly less confident, building to the total erosion of certainty. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Don't worry about it, Johnny. I'm 73% sure everything's going to be fine.

jokes.text: David tells Alexis there 'won't be anybody for you to flirtatiously boss around' — referring to Patrick
jokes.setup: David invites Alexis to help at the store, specifying Patrick won't be there
jokes.punchline: Alexis's motivation for helping is entirely to flirt with Patrick — which David has clocked perfectly
jokes.explanation: 'Flirtatiously boss around' is a precise and funny description of what Alexis was doing with Patrick. David reading her motivation exactly right is a good sibling dynamic joke.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I checked. I got down there with a flashlight and a mirror, and I looked around. Nothing happened.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I hate to break this to you, David, but there will always be somebody for me to flirtatiously boss around.'
jokes.setup: David has told Alexis she won't be able to flirtatiously boss anyone around at the store
jokes.punchline: Alexis's serene confidence that flirtatious bossing is a constant in her life — not specific to Patrick — is a wonderful self-aware character moment
jokes.explanation: Alexis's absolute certainty about this is perfect. The line is highly quotable and completely character-specific — it's a statement of worldview, not just a joke. 'There will always be somebody' suggests this is as reliable as gravity for Alexis. Excellent character integration and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland is like a... a burger. You know, it's fine, it's serviceable, but my wife knows the difference between a burger and a steak.

jokes.text: Alexis speculates that Patrick not asking for her number means he's 'either newly married or he's gay'
jokes.setup: Alexis is puzzled that Patrick hasn't asked for her number
jokes.punchline: For Alexis, a man not pursuing her requires a clinical explanation — and 'newly married or gay' is her full taxonomy of reasons a man might not be interested
jokes.explanation: This joke has massive rewatch value because the audience knows Patrick is gay and ends up with David. Alexis's 'or he's gay' throwaway becomes a planted revelation in retrospect. Even on first viewing, her inability to conceive of simple disinterest is very funny. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was supposed to have a luxurious soak in the marble soaking tub, but I passed out mid-conversation with Roland, and when I came to, he was using my bath water to rinse his feet.

jokes.text: David: 'He's a business major who wears straight leg, mid-range denim. He's not into me.'
jokes.setup: Alexis suggests maybe Patrick's eye is for someone else (implying David)
jokes.punchline: David dismisses the idea by citing Patrick's fashion choices — 'straight leg, mid-range denim' — as definitive proof of sexual orientation, which is both absurd sartorial logic and deeply David
jokes.explanation: An exceptional joke on multiple levels. First, 'straight leg, mid-range denim' as David's shorthand for a straight man is hilariously specific and completely David Rose — no one else on this show would use denim cut as a gaydar metric. Second, it's dramatically ironic because the audience will later know David is completely wrong about Patrick. Third, the self-deprecating 'He's not into me' at the end is a rare vulnerability from David, smuggled inside snobbery. Very high rewatch value, great economy, exceptional character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, if we ever got divorced, I'd fight you for custody of the store.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, David, you can't just take the store. I'd want visitation rights. And child support.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The store isn't a child.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, it's treated like one. You spend more time with it than you do with me.

jokes.text: David distracts Alexis from the Patrick conversation by telling her she missed a spot with the highlighter — she immediately looks
jokes.setup: Alexis is pushing David about Patrick
jokes.punchline: David's transparent deflection — 'I think you missed a spot on that page with the highlighter' — works immediately on Alexis, who falls for it
jokes.explanation: A quick, effective gag. David's obvious deflection and Alexis's immediate compliance is a nice sibling beat. Not a huge joke but lands cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know, Dad, if anything were to happen to you, Mom would be fine. She'd be sad, obviously, but she'd be fine. I mean, she's got me.

jokes.text: John confronts Moira: 'When were you gonna tell me about this?' Moira: 'Ah, we talked about it this morning, John! We laughed about it.' John: 'We didn't laugh about it. Because we didn't talk about it.'
jokes.setup: John confronts Moira about hiding the Roland incident
jokes.punchline: Moira insisting they 'laughed about it' while John calmly points out that laughing is impossible when the conversation never happened — his precision cutting through her revisionism
jokes.explanation: John's 'I would have remembered if you had said something that funny' is a quietly excellent line — he's acknowledging the absurdity of the situation would have been memorable while also calling out her lie. Good construction and character-specific delivery.

jokes.text: John: 'I would have remembered if you had said something that funny.'
jokes.setup: Moira insists they discussed it this morning and laughed about it
jokes.punchline: John's dry assurance that he'd remember something this objectively insane — delivered as logic — is perfect
jokes.explanation: One of John's best lines. The logic is airtight and the delivery is everything — he's not accusing, just noting that 'sleeping in Roland's bed' would have registered as memorable enough to recall. The understatement masks the absurdity perfectly. Strong craft across the board.

jokes.text: John asks what Moira is doing (cutting down on drinking); Moira: 'Unrelated, I assume?' To what? 'To you sleeping with Roland last night.' 'Okay. This joke has played itself out.'
jokes.setup: Moira is pouring something out — appearing to cut back on drinking
jokes.punchline: John's dry 'Unrelated, I assume?' followed by Moira's attempt to dismiss the entire episode as a 'joke that has played itself out' is perfect — she's trying to bury it with theatrical impatience
jokes.explanation: 'This joke has played itself out' is a great Moira line — she's trying to retroactively reclassify the incident as a joke that everyone has already enjoyed sufficiently. John's 'We didn't talk about it this morning!' in response is the perfect exasperated beat. Strong economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Moira says she owes an apology 'to you. And to sober people everywhere.'
jokes.setup: Moira is apologizing to John for the Roland incident
jokes.punchline: The addition of 'sober people everywhere' as a secondary category of wronged parties is magnificently theatrical — she's made her personal embarrassment into a public failing
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines of the episode. 'To sober people everywhere' is a perfectly Moira-ish escalation — she can't help but make everything grand, even her apologies. The phrase treats her drunken incident as an offense against an entire demographic. Only Moira Rose would apologize to sober people as a class. Highly quotable, excellent economy, exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: Moira pours out John's beer as a symbolic gesture of sobriety; John: 'Moira, that's my beer! You don't even drink beer!' Moira: 'It's symbolic, John.'
jokes.setup: Moira is making a dramatic gesture of cutting down on her drinking
jokes.punchline: She pours out John's beer — a drink she doesn't even consume — as her symbolic sacrifice; John's exasperation ('You don't even drink beer!') and Moira's serene 'It's symbolic, John' is a perfect exchange
jokes.explanation: A nearly perfect joke. The structure is immaculate: Moira's grand gesture of sobriety costs her nothing (she doesn't drink beer) while actually sacrificing something of John's. 'It's symbolic, John' delivered with complete serenity is the ideal button — she sees no contradiction. High quotability, excellent character integration, and the joke rewards on rewatch because you see the selfishness baked into her 'selfless' act.

jokes.text: Patrick returns to the store, David assumes his license has been revoked — Patrick: 'No, no. You're all good.' David: 'Okay. Um...'
jokes.setup: Patrick arrives at the store unexpectedly; David's first assumption is that something is wrong
jokes.punchline: David's default to catastrophe — assuming the license is revoked — and then his confused pause when told everything is fine, reveals his anxious relationship to good news
jokes.explanation: A quiet character joke — David's catastrophizing and then bewilderment at good news is consistent with his anxious character. Rewatch bonus because this is Patrick's return visit, which the audience knows is really about interest in David.

jokes.text: Patrick confesses he picked out the frame for the business license — 'they don't all come framed'
jokes.setup: Earlier, Patrick told David that all business licenses 'come framed' — we (and David) accepted this
jokes.punchline: Patrick admits he lied — he chose the frame himself — revealing a deliberate, if sweet, attempt to impress David from their very first meeting
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured callback that recontextualizes their entire first meeting. Patrick's embarrassed confession that he chose the frame — and the audience's recognition that this was his way of making a good impression — is both funny and the emotional foundation of the David-Patrick relationship. High rewatch bonus because every subsequent rewatch colors this moment with everything that follows. The joke is earned by the episode's structure.

jokes.text: David: 'So thank you for making it very clear that I will be making the creative decisions for the store. And I guess you can handle all the business stuff.'
jokes.setup: Patrick has confessed to framing the license in what David considers a corporate-looking frame
jokes.punchline: David immediately uses Patrick's confession to establish a business hierarchy that puts himself in creative control — pivoting embarrassment into authority with elegant speed
jokes.explanation: David's recovery from Patrick's confession is characteristically Davidesque — he pivots immediately to establishing creative dominance. The division of labor he proposes ('I'll do creative, you do business') is also the actual structure of their future relationship, giving it rewatch resonance.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'I'm gonna get the money.' Said with complete calm confidence after David expresses doubt
jokes.setup: David has expressed doubt about whether grant money will come through
jokes.punchline: Patrick's serene, unqualified confidence — 'I'm gonna get the money' — delivered without bluster or qualification is both funny and characterizing for Patrick as a foil to David's anxiety
jokes.explanation: Economy is nearly perfect — five words that establish Patrick as a calm, competent counterpoint to David's neurosis. Rewatch bonus because this confident assurance is the first glimpse of Patrick's essential character.

jokes.text: Roland arrives at John's room with beer, says 'Johnny, me and six of my closest friends were hoping we could have a little chat' — referring to the beer
jokes.setup: Roland arrives at John's door at night
jokes.punchline: His 'six of my closest friends' turns out to be a six-pack of beer — a grandiosely intimate description of a convenience store purchase
jokes.explanation: A warm Roland joke. 'Six of my closest friends' for a six-pack is a gentle, character-appropriate bit of whimsy. Roland's relationship with beer is like a friendship — he treats the reveal as completely natural.

jokes.text: Roland arrives late at night: 'Johnny, me and six of my closest friends were hoping we could have a little chat.' John: 'You're talking about the beer.'
jokes.setup: Roland shows up late at night and references 'six of my closest friends' hoping to chat with John.
jokes.punchline: He means the six-pack of beer. John immediately knows this.
jokes.explanation: Roland anthropomorphizing beer as 'six of my closest friends' is a good Roland joke — he approaches beer with the same warmth he approaches actual relationships. John's flat recognition of the reference is perfectly deadpan.

jokes.text: Roland is '99.999% sure nothing happened' — John: 'I'm 100% sure and I wasn't even there!'
jokes.setup: Roland is trying to reassure John that nothing happened with Moira
jokes.punchline: Roland's 99.999% (leaving a small margin of doubt) versus John's 100% certainty despite not being present is a perfect exchange — John's confidence exceeds the eyewitness's
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The structure is nearly perfect: Roland's 99.999% is supposed to be reassuring but mathematically leaves room for doubt; John's 100% certainty despite being absent is both a confidence in Moira AND a gentle rebuke to Roland for not being equally certain. 'I wasn't even there!' as the punchline — asserting more certainty than the witness — is a brilliant reversal. Highly quotable, great economy, excellent character integration for both men.

jokes.text: Roland: 'I am 99.999% sure that nothing happened between me and your wife.'
jokes.setup: Roland is trying to reassure John that nothing happened
jokes.punchline: The precision of '99.999%' — and the implied 0.001% of uncertainty — is deeply unhelpful as reassurance
jokes.explanation: The joke works because the specificity of '99.999%' makes the reassurance worse. Roland thinks the precision signals confidence; the audience understands it signals the opposite. The '0.001%' of doubt is doing enormous comedic work. Great character integration — only Roland would offer this as comfort.

jokes.text: Roland spent 35 minutes inspecting his own body for 'bite marks, back scratches, lipstick prints' — and found something on the bottom of his foot
jokes.setup: Roland is providing his forensic evidence that nothing happened
jokes.punchline: The specificity of his inspection checklist (bite marks, back scratches, lipstick prints) escalates beautifully, and 'something on the bottom of my foot' as the only finding — which he can't rule out as pre-existing — is perfectly absurd
jokes.explanation: This is a great joke. Roland's methodical self-examination is delivered with such sincerity — he genuinely did the inspection — and the escalating checklist (bite marks → back scratches → lipstick prints) is perfectly structured. The coup de grâce of 'something on the bottom of my foot' that 'may have been there before' is wonderfully absurd. Only Roland would bring forensic results to a conversation about marital fidelity. Very strong.

jokes.text: John tells Roland that 'my wife would never go out for a burger when she has a strong penchant for steak'
jokes.setup: John is reassuring Roland — and himself — that Moira would never be unfaithful
jokes.punchline: The burger/steak metaphor — Roland as the burger, John as the steak — is delivered with perfect seriousness; John is simultaneously insulting Roland and complimenting himself with complete dignity
jokes.explanation: A beautifully understated insult wrapped in a food metaphor. John delivers this with complete dignity — it's not cruel, it's just honest. The burger/steak framework is original, character-appropriate, and lands with maximum efficiency. 'A strong penchant for steak' is a particularly good construction.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Breakfast tomorrow, right?' — cheerfully ignoring the dismissal
jokes.setup: John has delivered a dignified speech insulting Roland and told him good night
jokes.punchline: Roland's breezy 'Breakfast tomorrow, right?' suggests he either missed the insult entirely or has completely absorbed it without offense — both options are funny
jokes.explanation: Roland's obliviousness to social cues is always funny. His cheerful pivot to 'breakfast tomorrow?' after being insulted and dismissed is perfectly Roland — utterly unflappable and immediately focused on the next meal.

jokes.text: John announces himself to Moira: 'Don't worry it's only me' — 'Ha ha, very droll, Mr. Rose'
jokes.setup: John enters Moira's room after the Roland incident
jokes.punchline: His 'Don't worry it's only me' — acknowledging what happened — and then escalating with 'just in case you were expecting somebody else'; Moira's 'very droll' signals she's accepting the ribbing
jokes.explanation: John making a gentle joke about the incident signals the couple's resilience — he can tease her because they're both fine. 'Very droll, Mr. Rose' is a good Moira line. Not a standout joke but warm and functional.

jokes.text: John asks about the hotel amenities with pointed subtext: 'High-pressured water, down pillows?' Moira: 'I'll never know. I had my one chance at a proletarian oasis and I squandered it, passed out in Roland's bed.'
jokes.setup: John is making indirect jokes about the Roland incident through 'innocent' questions about hotel amenities
jokes.punchline: Moira's response is both genuinely sad and funny — 'proletarian oasis' as her description of a standard hotel, and the punchline that she missed it by passing out in Roland's bed
jokes.explanation: 'A proletarian oasis' is a great Moira-ism — a Schitt's Creek hotel is her 'one chance' at basic luxury after losing their wealth. The phrase is funny and slightly poignant. The punchline delivery — she squandered it by passing out in Roland's bed — is a good button on the episode's central joke.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I had my one chance at a proletarian oasis and I squandered it, passed out on Roland's bed.'
jokes.setup: John asks about the hotel room amenities
jokes.punchline: Moira frames a drunken blackout in Roland's bed as a missed luxury opportunity — calling a conference hotel a 'proletarian oasis' is perfect Moira scale
jokes.explanation: 'Proletarian oasis' as a description of a conference hotel is wonderfully specific Moira vocabulary. She considers a standard hotel room a luxury experience by her previous standards — and she squandered even that. The self-pity is both genuine and comically overwrought. High quotability.

jokes.text: David bursts in asking 'Mom slept with Roland?' — Alexis confirms 'Yes. Apparently.'
jokes.setup: John and Moira are having an intimate moment of reconciliation
jokes.punchline: David and Alexis storming in and reducing the whole drama to a blunt transaction — 'Mom slept with Roland? Yes. Apparently.' — is a perfect comic interruption of the tender scene
jokes.explanation: Alexis's flat 'Yes. Apparently.' is a great deadpan beat — she's answering David as if confirming a restaurant order. The interruption of the reconciliation scene is well-timed.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I will not be tried and condemned by the likes of you two.'
jokes.setup: David and Alexis have walked in and are now sitting in judgment of Moira's RAMC incident
jokes.punchline: Moira frames her adult children's gossip as a formal tribunal — 'tried and condemned by the likes of you two' — with theatrical dignity
jokes.explanation: A strong Moira line. 'Tried and condemned by the likes of you two' is perfectly theatrical and completely in character — she elevates the family gossip into a legal proceeding. Very quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: David asks if there's going to be 'a custody battle over us' and whether they'll have to 'spend weekends over at Roland's'
jokes.setup: Moira has just denied that anything happened with Roland
jokes.punchline: David immediately plays out the divorce scenario — custody battle, weekend visits to Roland — as if the worst is assumed and planning should begin
jokes.explanation: David's catastrophizing takes a dark comedic turn as he games out custody arrangements. 'Weekends over at Roland's' as the nightmare scenario is both absurd and funny because it's exactly what David would find most unbearable.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I get it, Roland is the mayor, and it's very difficult to work with someone who you're attracted to.' David: 'Yeah. Like you and Ted?' Alexis: 'No! I'm making fun of mom and dad right now, David.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is (supposedly) teasing Moira about being attracted to Roland
jokes.punchline: David redirects the joke at Alexis's own history with Ted; Alexis's indignant 'I'm making fun of mom and dad right now, David' is perfect — she's outraged at the redirection of mockery
jokes.explanation: 'I'm making fun of mom and dad right now, David' is a great line — her outrage isn't that David is wrong about Ted, it's that he's disrupted her moment of mockery. The entitlement to mockery-time is very Alexis. Good sibling dynamic.

jokes.text: David to John and Moira: 'I just want you to know that no matter what anyone says, you will always be our first dad. Good night.'
jokes.setup: John and Moira have sent the kids to their rooms; David delivers his parting shot
jokes.punchline: 'You will always be our first dad' — implying John could be replaced — delivered with performative sincerity as David exits, is the perfect button on the entire Roland subplot
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke. 'You will always be our first dad' is structurally perfect — it acknowledges and dismisses the entire Roland scandal in one sentence, implies John could theoretically be succeeded by Roland as father, and is delivered with deadpan finality as David exits. The 'Good night' after is the ideal button. Only David Rose would find this exact phrasing. Extremely quotable, perfect economy, exceptional character integration. A genuine standout that will be remembered by viewers.

tmdb_overview: Moira and Roland find themselves in a compromising situation. Alexis drives a wedge between David and Patrick.
insight: Moira's wardrobe gags anchor 78.9 score with character-driven escalations through the episode.
